prompt: Build a Community Rescue & Clinic Billing Hub that enables a local health organization to manage patient encounters, community incident reports, and back-office financial settlement entirely on an internal network. Primary roles include Front Desk Clerk, Clinician, Billing Specialist, Quality Reviewer, Content Moderator, and System Administrator. In the Vue.js web interface, staff sign in with a username and password and land on a role-based dashboard: Front Desk can register a visit-patient, capture demographics and contact details, and verify an ID by recording document type and last four digits while the UI masks full identifiers by default and applies on-screen watermarking for sensitive views. Clinicians can look up prior appointments by date (MM/DD/YYYY) and quickly reopen historical visit summaries, with clear “archived” badges for older records. Community members or call-center staff can submit incident reports with an approximate location description (nearest cross streets, neighborhood) and attach photos/videos; the interface displays incidents and nearby shelters on a locally hosted map layer and provides a printable, turn-by-turn route sheet to the nearest resource, with permission-based visibility that can hide exact locations for minors or protected cases. Staff can search and discover incident and bulletin content using full-text search plus filters like time, popularity, favorites, and comments, and reviewers can promote items to ranked lists using configurable weighting to handle cold-start content. The backend uses Spring Boot to expose RESTful APIs consumed by the Vue.js client over internal HTTPS/TLS, and persists operational data in MySQL with strict validation and auditability. Billing and settlement support configurable service and package rules (e.g., office visit, lab panel, medication dispense), discounts (percentage or fixed, capped at $200.00 per invoice), and sales tax where applicable; bills auto-generate at visit close, reconcile mixed tender statuses (cash, check, card-on-file recorded as “external”), and enforce refund/void rules such as “refunds allowed within 30 days” and “void only before daily close at 11:00 PM.” Daily settlement reports and ledger exports produce local files only, and bulk exports above 500 rows require a second confirmation plus elevated role. Quality control runs a rules engine for cross-field checks (e.g., age vs. DOB), threshold and trend anomalies (e.g., unusually high test frequency within 7 days), duplicate detection using deterministic fingerprints, and blocking of missing/anomalous items unless a Quality Reviewer provides an override note; sampling review assigns 5% of closed visits for second-look and tracks corrective actions to closure. Security includes password hashing (bcrypt), encryption at rest for sensitive fields, CSRF/XSS/SQL injection protections, rate limiting (e.g., 10 login attempts per 10 minutes per workstation), allowlist/denylist controls, and risk scoring for anomalous logins and repeated export attempts; all create/update/approve/export actions write immutable audit logs with operator, timestamp, IP, and before/after values. Data consistency relies on optimistic locking and idempotency keys to prevent duplicate submissions, while backups run nightly to an on-prem directory with a 30-day retention and a documented restore test performed monthly; deletions are traceable and recorded as soft-delete with retention policies that archive inactive patient records after 7 years unless legally required longer.
